codeExamples.explanation: Query keys uniquely identify cached data. staleTime controls freshness. isLoading is true only on first load, while isFetching is true during any fetch (including background refetches).

codeExamples.title: Mutations and Cache Invalidation
codeExamples.explanation: Mutations handle writes. After success, invalidateQueries triggers a refetch of related data, or setQueryData updates the cache directly for instant UI updates.

codeExamples.title: Optimistic Updates
codeExamples.explanation: Optimistic updates show changes instantly. onMutate saves a snapshot and updates the cache. onError rolls back. onSettled refetches to ensure server and client are in sync.

notes: React Query replaces most useEffect data fetching patterns - it handles loading, errors, caching, and race conditions automatically
Query keys should include all variables that affect the fetched data (filters, pagination, IDs)
staleTime vs gcTime: staleTime controls when data is considered fresh (no refetch). gcTime controls when inactive cached data is garbage collected
React Query DevTools (ReactQueryDevtools) are essential for debugging - they show all cached queries, their status, and data
followUps: How do you implement infinite scroll / pagination with React Query?
What is the difference between staleTime and gcTime?
How does React Query handle race conditions compared to raw useEffect?
interviewTips: Explain why React Query is better than raw useEffect for data fetching: automatic caching, race condition handling, retry, and background refetch
The optimistic update pattern is a common interview topic - know the onMutate -> onError -> onSettled flow
Show understanding of query key design: include all parameters that affect the response

content.question: What are common React anti-patterns? How do you identify and fix them?
content.answer:
React anti-patterns are recurring code patterns that lead to bugs, poor performance, or unmaintainable code. Identifying and fixing them is a key skill that distinguishes experienced React developers.

State-related anti-patterns include: derived state that duplicates source data (compute it instead), unnecessary global state (most state should be local), and state that should be in the URL (filters, pagination, selected tabs). Component structure anti-patterns include: god components that do everything (break them up), prop drilling through many levels (use context or composition), and premature abstraction (don't extract components until you see clear patterns).

Performance anti-patterns include: creating objects and functions inside render (unstable references), unnecessary useEffect for derived values (compute during render), fetching data in useEffect without a library (missing caching, race conditions, error handling), and over-memoizing everything (adds complexity without measurable benefit).

The most insidious anti-pattern is using useEffect as a state synchronization mechanism. If you find yourself using useEffect to update state based on other state or props, you almost always can compute that value directly during rendering or handle it in the event handler that triggered the change.

codeExamples.title: Unnecessary useEffect (Most Common Anti-Pattern)
codeExamples.explanation: Most useEffect calls for state synchronization can be replaced by computing during render, handling in event handlers, or using the key prop for resets. useEffect should be reserved for synchronizing with external systems.

codeExamples.title: Component Structure Anti-Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: God components are hard to maintain and test. Prop drilling can often be solved with composition before reaching for context. Components defined inside render functions are recreated every render, causing full remounts.

codeExamples.title: State and Data Anti-Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: Derived state causes unnecessary complexity and sync bugs. URL state makes filters bookmarkable and shareable. State mutations cause silent bugs because React cannot detect changes to the same reference.

notes: The React docs explicitly say: 'You might not need an effect' - this is the single most common anti-pattern in React codebases
If you need useEffect to synchronize two pieces of state, it is almost always a sign you should restructure your code
Components defined inside render functions are a subtle but severe performance bug - they cause child remounting on every render
State in URLs (search params) is better than React state for filters, pagination, and tabs because it survives refreshes and is shareable
followUps: How do you refactor a god component into smaller pieces?
What is the difference between necessary and unnecessary useEffect calls?
interviewTips: Showing awareness of anti-patterns demonstrates real-world experience - interviewers value this highly
Know the 'You Might Not Need an Effect' principle from the React docs - it is the most impactful refactoring guideline
Discuss anti-patterns you have actually encountered and fixed in your projects - concrete examples are more convincing than theory

content.question: What is the difference between useLayoutEffect and useEffect? When should you use each?
content.answer:
useEffect runs asynchronously after the browser has painted the screen. useLayoutEffect runs synchronously after all DOM mutations but before the browser paints.

Timing:
1. React commits DOM changes
2. useLayoutEffect fires (synchronously) — browser is blocked
3. Browser paints
4. useEffect fires (asynchronously)

Use useLayoutEffect when:
- You need to read DOM layout and synchronously re-render to avoid visual flicker
- Measuring DOM dimensions that affect the initial render
- Tooltips, popovers, animations that depend on element position

Use useEffect for everything else — it is non-blocking and preferred for performance.

codeExamples.title: DOM Measurement with useLayoutEffect
codeExamples.explanation: useLayoutEffect fires synchronously before paint. For tooltip positioning, using useEffect would briefly show the tooltip in the wrong position.

notes: useLayoutEffect is equivalent to componentDidMount/componentDidUpdate in class components
Overusing useLayoutEffect blocks painting — only use when DOM measurement before paint is needed
In SSR, useLayoutEffect causes a warning — use useEffect with a mounted flag instead
React 18 adds useInsertionEffect for CSS-in-JS libraries that inject styles before layout
followUps: Why does useLayoutEffect cause a warning in SSR (Next.js)?
What is useInsertionEffect and when would you use it?
Can useLayoutEffect cause performance issues?
interviewTips: Lead with the timing: useLayoutEffect is sync before paint, useEffect is async after
Use tooltip/popover positioning as the canonical example — it is the clearest use case
Mention SSR warning — shows you have considered real-world scenarios

content.question: What is useImperativeHandle and when would you use it with forwardRef?
content.answer:
forwardRef allows a component to forward a ref to a child DOM element or component. useImperativeHandle customises what the parent sees when using that ref — instead of exposing the raw DOM node, you expose a controlled API.

Use cases:
- Exposing methods like focus(), reset(), scroll() without giving parents full DOM access
- Building reusable component libraries where internal implementation details should be hidden
- Imperative animations or video player controls

Best practice: prefer declarative props over refs. Use this pattern only when imperative access is truly necessary.

codeExamples.title: Custom Input with Exposed API
codeExamples.explanation: useImperativeHandle limits the ref to { focus, clear, getValue }. The parent cannot accidentally access internal DOM properties.

notes: forwardRef is required to receive a ref passed from a parent as a prop
useImperativeHandle's second argument (factory) runs when deps change
In React 19, ref is a regular prop — forwardRef is no longer needed
Over-use of refs/imperative code is an anti-pattern — most things should be declarative
followUps: When would you use useImperativeHandle over a controlled component pattern?
What changes in React 19 regarding refs?
What is the difference between a ref and a callback ref?
interviewTips: Show the encapsulation benefit — parent gets { focus, clear } not the whole DOM node
Mention React 19 deprecating forwardRef — shows you follow React roadmap
Balance with 'prefer declarative' — shows you know when NOT to use it

content.question: What is automatic batching in React 18? How does it differ from React 17?
content.answer:
Batching: React groups multiple state updates into a single re-render for performance.

React 17: batching only worked inside React event handlers. Updates inside setTimeout, Promises, or native event listeners triggered separate re-renders.

React 18: automatic batching applies everywhere — inside setTimeout, Promises, fetch callbacks, and native event listeners.

flushSync: opt out of batching to force synchronous updates immediately (use sparingly — it hurts performance).

codeExamples.title: React 17 vs React 18 Batching
codeExamples.explanation: React 18 batches state updates even in async contexts. This can improve performance but may require flushSync in rare cases where immediate DOM updates are needed.

codeExamples.title: flushSync to Opt Out
codeExamples.explanation: flushSync forces React to flush state updates synchronously, allowing you to read the updated DOM immediately. The chat scroll pattern is the canonical use case.

notes: Automatic batching in React 18 reduces unnecessary re-renders in async code
createRoot (React 18) enables automatic batching; ReactDOM.render (legacy) does not
flushSync bypasses batching and should be used sparingly
Batching applies within a single call stack — not across separate async operations
followUps: What is the difference between createRoot and ReactDOM.render?
When would you need flushSync?
Does automatic batching affect the order of state updates?
interviewTips: React 18 automatic batching is a common interview topic — know the before/after
Show the setTimeout example — it is the most intuitive demonstration
Mention flushSync as the escape hatch and give the chat scroll example

content.question: What are useTransition and useDeferredValue in React 18? When would you use each?
content.answer:
Both are React 18 concurrent features that mark state updates as non-urgent, allowing urgent updates (typing, clicking) to remain responsive.

useTransition: wraps a state update as a 'transition' — non-urgent. React may interrupt it to handle urgent updates. Returns [isPending, startTransition].

useDeferredValue: defers a value update, showing old value while computing the new one. Like debounce but React-driven.

Difference:
- useTransition: you control which state update is deferred (you own the state)
- useDeferredValue: you defer a value you received (you do not own the state setter, e.g., from props)

codeExamples.title: useTransition for Search
codeExamples.explanation: The input (urgent) updates immediately. The expensive list filter (non-urgent) is deferred. isPending shows a loading indicator during transition.

codeExamples.title: useDeferredValue
codeExamples.explanation: useDeferredValue keeps showing the old value while computing the new one. The opacity dim provides visual feedback that results are stale.

notes: Both require React 18 and concurrent rendering (createRoot)
Transitions are lower priority than urgent updates — React may skip them to stay responsive
useDeferredValue behaves similarly to debounce but is React-driven, not time-based
Components receiving deferred values should be wrapped in React.memo for optimization
followUps: What is the difference between useTransition and useDeferredValue?
When would you choose useDeferredValue over debounce?
What happens if a transition takes too long in React 18?
interviewTips: Lead with the urgent vs non-urgent distinction — that is the core mental model
useTransition (own the state setter) vs useDeferredValue (receive a value from props)
Show the isPending indicator — it demonstrates how to give users feedback

content.question: What is React Fiber? Why was it introduced and what does it enable?
content.answer:
React Fiber is the complete rewrite of React's core reconciliation algorithm, introduced in React 16.

Before Fiber (Stack Reconciler): rendering was synchronous and non-interruptible. A deep tree update would block the main thread until complete, causing dropped frames and janky UIs.

Fiber introduced:
1. Incremental rendering: work can be split into chunks and spread across frames
2. Priority-based scheduling: urgent updates (input, animation) can interrupt non-urgent work
3. Ability to pause, abort, or reuse work
4. Better support for error boundaries and async rendering

A Fiber is a JavaScript object representing a unit of work — each component instance has a fiber node forming a linked list tree.

codeExamples.title: Fiber Work Loop (Conceptual)
codeExamples.explanation: Fiber uses a linked list instead of the call stack, making it possible to pause and resume work. The render phase is interruptible; the commit phase is not.

codeExamples.title: Fiber Enables Concurrent Features
codeExamples.explanation: Fiber's work scheduling enables all modern React features. Priority levels let React interrupt low-priority work to handle user interactions immediately.

notes: React Fiber introduced the concept of 'double buffering' — the current tree and the work-in-progress tree
The commit phase (applying DOM changes) is always synchronous and uninterruptible
React uses a Scheduler package (separate) for priority-based work scheduling
Understanding Fiber is important for understanding why React 18 features work
followUps: What is the difference between the render phase and the commit phase?
Why can the render phase be interrupted but not the commit phase?
What is double buffering in React Fiber?
interviewTips: Lead with the problem Fiber solved: synchronous blocking renders
Explain that Fiber is just a unit of work in a linked list — not magic
Connect Fiber to concrete features: Suspense, transitions, error boundaries

content.question: What is the Compound Components pattern in React? When would you use it?
content.answer:
Compound Components is a pattern where multiple components work together to achieve shared state and behavior, with a flexible, declarative API.

Instead of a single complex component with many props, you expose multiple sub-components that communicate via Context.

Advantages:
- Inversion of control — users decide how to arrange the sub-components
- No props drilling
- Flexible composition
- Familiar API (like HTML select/option)

Examples: Tab/TabPanel, Accordion/AccordionItem, Select/Option, Dropdown/DropdownItem

codeExamples.title: Accordion Compound Component
codeExamples.explanation: Context provides shared state. Sub-components access it via useContext. Users control layout and ordering — the parent just manages state.

notes: Sub-components are often attached as static properties: Tab.Panel, Tab.Header
Context is the communication mechanism between compound components
This pattern gives library consumers full control over rendering
Headless UI libraries (Radix, Headless UI) use this pattern extensively
followUps: How is compound components different from a config-prop approach?
What are the downsides of compound components?
How do libraries like Radix UI use this pattern?
interviewTips: Use Accordion or Tabs as examples — they are intuitive and well-known
Emphasize inversion of control — users arrange sub-components as they want
Mention Radix UI as a real-world library that uses this pattern

content.question: What does React Strict Mode do? Why does it double-invoke component functions?
content.answer:
React.StrictMode is a development-only tool that:

1. Double-invokes component render functions, useState initializers, and reducer functions to surface side effects in non-pure functions
2. Warns about deprecated APIs (componentWillMount, findDOMNode, etc.)
3. Detects unexpected side effects
4. In React 18: mounts, unmounts, and remounts every component to simulate resilience to future features

Double-invocation is only in development. It is intentional — it surfaces bugs where render is not pure. If your component only works correctly when rendered once, it has a bug.

Strict Mode has no effect on the production build.

codeExamples.title: StrictMode and Double-Invoke
codeExamples.explanation: React 18's StrictMode mounts/unmounts components to verify cleanup works. If your app breaks from this, your useEffect cleanup is missing or incorrect.

notes: StrictMode is a development tool only — no impact on production builds
The double-mount in React 18 Strict Mode is intentional — helps surface missing cleanup
Create React App and Next.js enable Strict Mode by default
If useEffect fires twice and causes bugs, the cleanup function is missing
followUps: Why does React 18 StrictMode mount components twice?
Does StrictMode affect performance in production?
How do you handle the double-mount issue with third-party integrations?
interviewTips: Stress that it is development-only — a common misconception is that it slows production
The double-mount is the most confusing aspect — explain it is intentional bug detection
Connect to useEffect cleanup — missing cleanup is the #1 cause of double-mount issues

content.question: How do you implement accessibility in React applications?
content.answer:
Key accessibility practices in React:

1. Semantic HTML: use proper elements (button, nav, main, article) instead of div with onClick
2. ARIA attributes: aria-label, aria-describedby, aria-live, role when semantic HTML is insufficient
3. Focus management: move focus programmatically after modals open/close
4. Keyboard navigation: all interactive elements must be keyboard accessible
5. Color contrast: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for text
6. Alt text: all images need meaningful alt attributes
7. Form labels: every input needs an associated label

React-specific: jsx uses className (not class), htmlFor (not for). React 16+ uses aria-* attributes natively.

codeExamples.title: Accessible Modal with Focus Management
codeExamples.explanation: Focus must move into the modal on open and return to the trigger on close. role=dialog and aria-modal=true announce the modal to screen readers.

codeExamples.title: Accessible Form and Live Regions
codeExamples.explanation: aria-live announces dynamic content changes. aria-describedby links related text. role=alert for errors. htmlFor is React's equivalent of HTML for attribute.

notes: Use eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y to catch accessibility issues at lint time
React 18 Offscreen (experimental) must not render hidden content accessibly
Radix UI and Headless UI provide accessible primitives out of the box
WCAG 2.1 AA is the standard for most applications
followUps: How do you test React components for accessibility?
What is a focus trap and how do you implement it?
What is the difference between aria-label and aria-labelledby?
interviewTips: Semantic HTML first — ARIA is a last resort when native elements cannot be used
Focus management in modals is the most technically complex a11y topic — know it well
Mention eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y — shows you enforce a11y at the tooling level

content.question: What is code splitting in React? How do you implement it with React.lazy?
content.answer:
Code splitting divides the JavaScript bundle into smaller chunks loaded on demand. This reduces initial bundle size and improves Time to Interactive.

React.lazy() dynamically imports a component, integrating with React's Suspense for loading states.

Splitting strategies:
1. Route-based: each route is a separate chunk (most impactful)
2. Component-based: lazy-load heavy components (charts, rich editors)
3. Library-based: split large third-party libraries

Bundler support: Webpack, Vite, and Rollup all support dynamic import() for code splitting.

codeExamples.title: Route-Based Code Splitting
codeExamples.explanation: Each route becomes a separate JS chunk. The Suspense fallback shows while the chunk loads. Initial bundle only includes the entry point code.

codeExamples.title: Component-Level Splitting and Error Handling
codeExamples.explanation: Always wrap lazy components in both Suspense (loading) and ErrorBoundary (network failure). Preloading on hover further improves perceived performance.

notes: React.lazy only works with default exports — named exports require re-exporting
Suspense must be an ancestor of the lazy component, not a sibling
Multiple lazy components can share one Suspense boundary
next/dynamic in Next.js is the equivalent with additional SSR options
followUps: How does code splitting reduce Time to Interactive?
What happens if a lazy-loaded component fails to load?
How do you preload a code-split chunk?
interviewTips: Route-based splitting is the highest-impact strategy — start there
Always pair React.lazy with Suspense AND ErrorBoundary in production
Preloading on hover is a nice performance detail that impresses interviewers

content.question: What is server-side rendering (SSR) in React? What is hydration and what causes hydration mismatches?
content.answer:
SSR: the server renders the React component tree to HTML, sends it to the client. Users see content before JavaScript loads.

SSG: pages are rendered at build time and served as static HTML.

Hydration: React attaches event listeners and state to the server-rendered HTML without re-rendering from scratch. React compares server HTML with what it would render on the client.

Hydration mismatch: if the server-rendered HTML differs from what React would render on the client, React warns and may re-render from scratch. Common causes:
- Date/time rendering (different between server and client)
- Random IDs or Math.random()
- User-agent detection
- Accessing window/document on the server

codeExamples.title: SSR Hydration in Next.js
codeExamples.explanation: getServerSideProps runs per request; getStaticProps runs at build. RSC in App Router always runs on the server with no client bundle.

codeExamples.title: Hydration Mismatch Causes and Fixes
codeExamples.explanation: Hydration mismatches cause React to re-render from scratch on the client (slow). suppressHydrationWarning, useEffect-gated access, and useId solve the most common causes.

notes: React 18's hydrateRoot is the new hydration API (replaces ReactDOM.hydrate)
Partial Hydration and Selective Hydration allow hydrating only interactive parts
React Server Components (RSC) never hydrate — they have no client JS
useId generates stable IDs that match between server and client
followUps: What is the difference between SSR and SSG?
How does React Server Components differ from SSR?
What causes a hydration mismatch and how do you fix it?
interviewTips: Lead with why SSR: faster First Contentful Paint and better SEO
Hydration mismatch is a common production bug — show you know the causes
useId is the elegant React 18 solution to the ID mismatch problem

content.question: What are React Synthetic Events? How does React's event system work?
content.answer:
React wraps native browser events in a SyntheticEvent object that normalizes cross-browser behavior and provides a consistent API.

In React 16 and earlier: React attached all event listeners to the document root using event delegation — one listener at the top for efficiency.

In React 17+: event delegation was moved to the React root container (not document). This allows multiple React versions on the same page (micro-frontends).

SyntheticEvent pooling was removed in React 17 — previously events were pooled and properties reset to null after the handler, which surprised developers who accessed events asynchronously.

codeExamples.title: Synthetic Events and nativeEvent
codeExamples.explanation: In React 17+, synthetic events are no longer pooled — you can access event properties asynchronously. Listeners are on the root container, not document.

codeExamples.title: Stopping Propagation with Synthetic vs Native Events
codeExamples.explanation: In React 17, stopPropagation works correctly because events are scoped to the root container. Passive event listeners must be added via useEffect for touch performance.

notes: Synthetic events implement the W3C spec for cross-browser consistency
Event pooling was removed in React 17 — previously caused confusing async bugs
React 17 change to root delegation enables micro-frontend architectures
onScroll does not bubble in React (by design)
followUps: What was event pooling and why was it removed in React 17?
Why did React 17 move event delegation from document to the root container?
How do you add passive event listeners in React?
interviewTips: React 17 event pooling removal is a common gotcha question — know it was removed
Root delegation change in React 17 matters for micro-frontends and portals
Show passive event listeners with useEffect — performance-aware detail

content.question: How does the children prop work in React? What is React.Children and when would you use it?
content.answer:
The children prop receives anything rendered between a component's opening and closing tags — JSX, strings, arrays, or null.

React.Children utilities:
- React.Children.map: iterate over children (handles single, multiple, null)
- React.Children.count: count children
- React.Children.forEach: side effects on each child
- React.Children.only: assert exactly one child
- React.cloneElement: clone a child element with additional props

Modern alternative: React 18 recommends the Context API or render props over React.Children manipulation, which can be fragile. Explicit slot props are often cleaner.

codeExamples.title: React.cloneElement for Enhancing Children
codeExamples.explanation: cloneElement injects name, checked, and onChange into each Radio child. Users declare Radio components naturally without specifying shared props.

codeExamples.title: Slot Pattern (Modern Alternative)
codeExamples.explanation: Explicit slot props (header, sidebar, footer) are more predictable than React.Children manipulation — components are easy to understand without reading implementation.

notes: React.Children.map handles null/undefined children gracefully; Array.map does not
cloneElement is fragile — it breaks if child types change; context is often better
React.Children utilities are less common in modern React — explicit slots or context preferred
In React 19, ref forwarding is simplified and some Children utilities are being deprecated
followUps: What is the difference between React.Children.map and Array.from(children).map?
When would you use cloneElement vs Context?
What is the slot pattern in React?
interviewTips: Show cloneElement with a real use case like RadioGroup — it is the classic example
Mention that Context and explicit slot props are the modern preference over Children API
React.Children.only() is useful for enforcing single child constraint in component APIs

title: Advanced Form Patterns and React Hook Form

content.question: What are the advantages of React Hook Form over controlled form state? How do you integrate validation?
content.answer:
React Hook Form (RHF) uses uncontrolled inputs by default (native form elements with refs), avoiding re-renders on every keystroke.

Advantages over manual useState:
- Fewer re-renders: only re-renders on submit or field validation, not every keypress
- Less boilerplate: register(field) replaces value/onChange pairs
- Built-in validation: required, min/max, pattern, custom rules
- Schema validation: integrates with Zod, Yup via @hookform/resolvers
- Performance: large forms with 100+ fields remain responsive

use-case: any form larger than 2-3 fields benefits from RHF.

codeExamples.title: React Hook Form with Zod
codeExamples.explanation: zodResolver bridges Zod schema validation to RHF. register() injects name, ref, onChange, onBlur. Errors are typed from the Zod schema.

notes: RHF uses uncontrolled inputs by default — no re-render on each keystroke
Controller component is needed for third-party controlled inputs (Select, DatePicker)
watch() subscribes to field changes and does re-render — use sparingly
Formik is an alternative but generally slower due to controlled inputs
followUps: How does React Hook Form reduce re-renders compared to useState?
When would you use the Controller component in React Hook Form?
What is the difference between register() and Controller in RHF?
interviewTips: Uncontrolled vs controlled is the key performance distinction — lead with that
Show Zod integration — TypeScript-first schema validation is standard in modern apps
Mention mode: 'onBlur' vs 'onChange' validation timing trade-offs

content.question: What are useId and useSyncExternalStore hooks in React 18?
content.answer:
useId: generates a unique, stable ID that is consistent between server and client renders. Solves the hydration mismatch problem from Math.random() or incrementing counters.

Used for: linking form inputs to labels, ARIA attributes.

useSyncExternalStore: subscribes to external data stores (non-React state) in a concurrent-safe way. It prevents UI tearing — the inconsistency that could occur if different parts of the UI read from an external store at different times during a concurrent render.

Used for: Redux (uses it internally), browser APIs like window.innerWidth, Zustand.

codeExamples.title: useId for Form Accessibility
codeExamples.explanation: useId generates IDs like :r0:, :r1: — stable across server and client. You can derive multiple IDs from one call by appending suffixes.

codeExamples.title: useSyncExternalStore
codeExamples.explanation: useSyncExternalStore prevents UI tearing in concurrent mode by ensuring the subscription, snapshot, and server snapshot are all consistent within a single render.

notes: useId IDs start with : and contain letters — do not use as CSS selectors
useSyncExternalStore replaces the manual subscribe/unsubscribe pattern in useEffect
Zustand and Redux use useSyncExternalStore under the hood in React 18
The server snapshot parameter is required for SSR to work correctly
followUps: What is UI tearing and how does useSyncExternalStore prevent it?
Why is Math.random() not safe for generating React IDs?
How does useSyncExternalStore differ from useEffect + useState for subscriptions?
interviewTips: useId is simple but shows SSR awareness — a common interview trap
useSyncExternalStore is advanced but shows deep React 18 knowledge
Mention Redux and Zustand using it internally — connects theory to practice

content.question: What are the different approaches to animations in React? What is Framer Motion?
content.answer:
Animation approaches in React:

1. CSS transitions/animations: simplest, GPU-accelerated, no JS overhead
2. CSS Modules + className toggle: add/remove classes to trigger animations
3. Inline style transitions: animate style properties directly (limited, no keyframes)
4. Framer Motion: declarative, component-based animation library with physics, variants, AnimatePresence
5. React Spring: physics-based animations with interpolation
6. GSAP: powerful for complex timeline animations

Framer Motion is the most popular React-specific library. It uses the Web Animations API and CSS transforms under the hood for 60fps performance.

codeExamples.title: Framer Motion Basics
codeExamples.explanation: Variants define named animation states. staggerChildren cascades animations through children automatically.

codeExamples.title: AnimatePresence for Exit Animations
codeExamples.explanation: AnimatePresence keeps components mounted until exit animation completes. layout prop automatically animates height/width changes with FLIP technique.

notes: Prefer transform and opacity for animations — they are GPU-composited and do not cause reflows
AnimatePresence requires a unique key prop on animated children
Framer Motion uses the will-change CSS property automatically for performance
prefers-reduced-motion: always respect user accessibility preferences
followUps: What CSS properties should you avoid animating for performance?
What is the FLIP animation technique?
How do you respect the prefers-reduced-motion media query?
interviewTips: Show AnimatePresence — it is unique to Framer Motion and impresses interviewers
Mention transform/opacity as the performance-safe properties to animate
Connect to prefers-reduced-motion for accessibility — shows holistic thinking

content.question: What are headless components in React? How do they differ from UI component libraries?
content.answer:
Headless components (also called headless UI) separate behavior and accessibility logic from visual presentation. They provide the logic (state, keyboard handling, ARIA) but delegate all rendering to the consumer.

Implementation approaches:
1. Custom hooks: return state and event handlers — consumer renders whatever they want
2. Render props: pass render function that receives state
3. Compound components: multiple sub-components sharing state via context

Libraries: Radix UI, Headless UI (Tailwind Labs), Tanstack Table, Downshift

Vs traditional component libraries (MUI, Ant Design): headless gives full styling control at the cost of more rendering code.

codeExamples.title: Headless Toggle Hook
codeExamples.explanation: The hook encapsulates state and ARIA. Different consumers can render entirely different UIs with the same behavior.

codeExamples.title: Headless Combobox (Search + Select)
codeExamples.explanation: Radix provides all accessibility and keyboard behavior. You write all the CSS. This is the headless component library model.

notes: Headless components have no default styles — ideal for projects with custom design systems
Tanstack Table is the most popular headless table library — complete data grid logic, no UI
The tradeoff: more flexibility but more rendering code compared to opinionated UI libraries
getProps pattern (getToggleProps) is the most flexible headless API design
followUps: What is the difference between Radix UI and shadcn/ui?
When would you choose a headless library over MUI or Ant Design?
What is the getProps pattern in headless components?
interviewTips: Explain the separation: logic (hooks/behavior) from presentation (JSX/styles)
Mention Radix UI and shadcn/ui — they are the current industry standard approach
Show the custom hook pattern — it is the simplest form of headless component

content.question: How do you profile and optimize React application performance?
content.answer:
Profiling workflow:
1. React DevTools Profiler: record interactions, view flame graph, find slow renders
2. Profiler API: programmatically measure component render time
3. Chrome Performance tab: measure JavaScript execution, painting, layout
4. why-did-you-render: library that logs unexpected re-renders

Common optimizations:
- React.memo: prevent re-renders when props are unchanged
- useMemo/useCallback: stabilize references
- Virtualization: react-window/react-virtual for long lists
- Code splitting: lazy load heavy components
- Key optimization: stable, unique keys for list items

codeExamples.title: Profiler API and React DevTools
codeExamples.explanation: baseDuration vs actualDuration shows memoization effectiveness. If actualDuration << baseDuration, React.memo is working well.

codeExamples.title: Finding and Fixing Unnecessary Re-renders
codeExamples.explanation: New objects/functions as props bypass React.memo because they are new references. useMemo and useCallback create stable references.

notes: React DevTools Profiler only works in development — use production profiling for realistic numbers
The Profiler API works in production (with minor overhead) for real user monitoring
Premature optimization is harmful — profile first, then optimize
Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS) measure user-perceived performance, not just render time
followUps: What is the difference between actualDuration and baseDuration in the Profiler?
How do you profile React in production?
What is the relationship between React performance and Core Web Vitals?
interviewTips: Show familiarity with React DevTools Profiler — it is the first tool to reach for
Demonstrate understanding of reference equality as the root cause of re-renders
Mention Web Vitals — shows you care about user-perceived performance, not just JS metrics

content.question: What is state colocation in React? When should you lift state up?
content.answer:
State colocation means keeping state as close to where it is used as possible. The opposite is unnecessary global state — putting everything in a context or store when it only serves one component.

Principle: state that is only needed by one component lives in that component. State needed by siblings lives in their closest common ancestor (lift up).

When to lift up:
- Two siblings need to share the same piece of state
- A parent component needs to control child behavior based on state

When NOT to lift up:
- State is local to a single component (UI state: open/closed, hover, etc.)
- Lifting would cause unnecessary re-renders of unrelated components

Lifting too high (global store for local UI state) is an anti-pattern that causes performance issues and code complexity.

codeExamples.title: Lifting State Up
codeExamples.explanation: The parent owns the celsius state as the single source of truth. Both inputs derive from it — Fahrenheit is computed, not stored.

codeExamples.title: State Colocation: Keep State Local
codeExamples.explanation: UI state (menu open, modal, tabs) should stay local. Global store is for truly shared state. URL is the right place for shareable filter state.

notes: The rule: move state as close to where it is needed as possible
Lifting too high causes unnecessary re-renders of unrelated components
URL (useSearchParams) is underused — ideal for filters, pagination, selected items
React team recommends: local state first, then context, then external store
followUps: What is the difference between UI state and server state?
When would you put state in the URL instead of React state?
What are the performance implications of lifting state too high?
interviewTips: Colocation is a fundamental React principle — shows architectural thinking
Show the temperature converter — it is the React docs' canonical lift-up example
URL state is the most underused pattern — mention it for brownie points

content.question: What are the key concurrent features introduced in React 18?
content.answer:
React 18 introduced concurrent rendering, which allows React to prepare multiple versions of the UI simultaneously and pause, abandon, or resume work based on priority.

Key changes:
1. createRoot: new root API that enables concurrent features
2. Automatic Batching: state updates batched everywhere (setTimeout, fetch, native events)
3. useTransition and useDeferredValue: mark low-priority updates
4. Streaming SSR with Suspense: server streams HTML progressively
5. Selective Hydration: React hydrates interactive parts first
6. React Server Components (in Next.js App Router): components that run only on server

Enabling: replace ReactDOM.render with createRoot().

codeExamples.title: createRoot and Concurrent Features
codeExamples.explanation: createRoot is the gateway to all React 18 concurrent features. Streaming SSR with Suspense sends HTML progressively — users see the page shell before data loads.

codeExamples.title: Selective Hydration
codeExamples.explanation: Multiple Suspense boundaries allow independent streaming and hydration. React hydrates whichever part the user interacts with first.

notes: Concurrent features are opt-in via createRoot — upgrading from ReactDOM.render is backward compatible
React Server Components are a separate concept from SSR — RSC never hydrate
Streaming SSR requires a streaming-capable server (not static file hosting)
React 19 builds on these features with further improvements to RSC and form handling
followUps: What is the difference between React Server Components and SSR?
How does selective hydration improve user experience?
What is streaming SSR and how does it differ from traditional SSR?
interviewTips: createRoot is the entry point — without it, React 18 features are disabled
Streaming SSR + selective hydration is React's answer to improving Time to Interactive
RSC vs SSR distinction is a common interview trap — RSC = no client bundle, SSR = hydrates

content.question: How would you architect a large-scale React application? What are the key decisions?
content.answer:
Key architectural decisions for large React apps:

1. State management: local state → Context → Zustand/Redux based on complexity
2. Data fetching: React Query / SWR for server state (caching, invalidation, optimistic updates)
3. Routing: React Router v6+ or Next.js App Router
4. Component architecture: atomic design, feature-based folders
5. Code splitting: route-based, then component-based for heavy modules
6. Performance: virtualization for large lists, memoization for expensive computations
7. Styling: CSS Modules, Tailwind, or CSS-in-JS based on team and requirements
8. Testing: Jest + React Testing Library (unit), Playwright/Cypress (e2e)
9. Error handling: error boundaries per feature, global error reporting

codeExamples.title: Feature-Based Folder Structure
codeExamples.code:
// Scalable folder structure
src/
  features/           # Feature modules (domain-driven)
    auth/
      components/     # Auth UI components
      hooks/          # useAuth, usePermissions
      store/          # Auth state (Zustand slice)
      api/            # Auth API calls
      types.ts
    products/
      components/
      hooks/
      store/
      api/
    orders/
  shared/             # Truly reusable across features
    components/       # Button, Modal, Table
    hooks/            # useDebounce, useLocalStorage
    utils/
    types/
  pages/              # Route components (thin — import features)
  lib/
    api-client.ts     # Axios instance
    query-client.ts   # React Query config
  App.tsx

// Key principles:
// 1. Features are self-contained (can be deleted without breaking others)
// 2. Shared code is truly shared (used by 2+ features)
// 3. Pages are thin — they compose feature components
// 4. Collocate tests with components (__tests__/ or *.test.tsx)

// Import boundaries (enforce with eslint-plugin-import)
// features/auth can NOT import from features/products
// features/* can import from shared/*
// pages/* can import from features/* and shared/*
codeExamples.explanation: Feature-based structure scales better than layer-based (components/, services/, hooks/). Each feature owns its components, hooks, and state.

codeExamples.title: Data Layer Architecture
codeExamples.explanation: Layered data architecture: API functions → custom hooks → thin components. React Query handles caching, background refetch, loading/error states.

notes: Feature-based structure scales better than layer-based for teams over 5 developers
Server state (React Query) and client state (Zustand) should be kept separate
Establish import boundaries early — prevent features from depending on each other
Document architecture decisions in ADRs (Architecture Decision Records)
followUps: How would you handle authentication state in a large React app?
When would you choose Redux over Zustand?
How do you prevent circular dependencies between features?
interviewTips: System design questions test architectural thinking — show trade-offs, not just choices
Feature-based folders is the industry consensus for 2024+ React projects
Distinguish server state (React Query) from client state (Zustand/Redux) — a maturity indicator
